plainMeaning: Merutuṅgasūri declares that anyone who meditates in the heart on Lord Pārśva — pictured himself deep in meditation — is certain to attain every spiritual success.
recitation: śrī merutuṅga sūrīndraḥ, śrīmat-pārśva-prabhoḥ puraḥ; dhyāna-sthitaṁ hṛdi dhyāyan, sarva-siddhiṁ labhe dhruvam.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit śrī, from √śri 'to resort to, to be radiant'; denotes splendour, prosperity, and an honorific prefix.
wordByWord.notes: Here it dignifies the ācārya's name.

wordByWord.etymology: Proper name: meru (the cosmic mountain) + tuṅga 'lofty, high' — 'lofty as Mount Meru'.
wordByWord.notes: The hymn's attributed author; the same name closes v.11.

wordByWord.gloss: lord among ācāryas, chief of teachers (nominative singular)
wordByWord.etymology: sūri 'sage, ācārya' + indra 'lord, chief' (sūri + indra → sūrīndra); nominative -aḥ.
wordByWord.notes: Honorific apposition to Merutuṅga: 'Merutuṅga, the foremost of sūris'.

wordByWord.gloss: of the glorious Lord Pārśva (genitive singular)
wordByWord.etymology: śrīmat 'glorious, illustrious' + pārśva (the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara) + prabhu 'lord, master'; genitive -oḥ.
wordByWord.notes: Governed by puraḥ ('before the Lord'). The printed form shows ārśva for pārśva — read as pārśva-prabhoḥ; flagged as a book/OCR variant.

wordByWord.gloss: in front of, before, in the presence of
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit indeclinable (avyaya) puraḥ 'in front', from pur- 'before'; governs the genitive.
wordByWord.notes: Locates the act of meditation 'in the presence of' the Lord.

wordByWord.etymology: dhyāna 'meditation' (√dhyai 'to meditate') + sthita 'standing, established' (√sthā 'to stand'); accusative -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative object of dhyāyan — the meditator contemplates Pārśva who is himself in dhyāna.

wordByWord.etymology: Locative singular of hṛd 'heart' (Sanskrit hṛdaya); locative hṛdi.
wordByWord.notes: Printed as 'hadi' without the ṛ-vowel; read as hṛdi. Echoes the heart-lotus / mantra-king-in-the-heart motif.

wordByWord.etymology: Present participle of √dhyai 'to meditate', nominative masculine singular dhyāyan.
wordByWord.notes: Agrees with the subject Merutuṅga-sūrīndraḥ; the doubling with dhyāna-sthitaṁ is deliberate.
Object of labhe; spans worldly success and ultimate spiritual fulfilment.

wordByWord.etymology: First person singular present middle of √labh 'to obtain, receive'.
wordByWord.notes: First-person voice: the poet speaks of his own attainment, modelling it for the reciter.

wordByWord.etymology: Neuter of dhruva 'fixed, firm' (√dhṛ 'to hold'), used adverbially: 'fixedly → certainly'.

literalTranslation: Śrī Merutuṅga, lord among ācāryas, before the glorious Lord Pārśva, meditating in the heart upon (him who is) absorbed in meditation, surely obtains all attainment.
idiomaticTranslation: Śrī Merutuṅgasūri, foremost of teachers, standing before the glorious Lord Pārśva and contemplating within his heart that same Lord rapt in meditation, assuredly wins every accomplishment.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The closing verse seals the hymn with the poet's own signature act of devotion. Having taught the bīja-mantras, the inner channels, and the litany of fears across the preceding verses, Merutuṅga now simply does what he has prescribed: he sits 'before' the Lord (puraḥ) and meditates 'in the heart' (hṛdi). The verse is built on a single, elegant reflexivity — the meditator (dhyāyan) contemplates the Lord who is himself 'established in meditation' (dhyāna-sthitaṁ). The worshipper does not merely picture an image; he mirrors the Lord's own meditative stillness, so that subject and object converge in a shared dhyāna. This is why the heart (hṛdi) is named rather than the eyes: the Pārśva visualized is the inner, mantra-borne Lord of the earlier verses, not the external icon. The first-person verb labhe ('I gain') is striking in a colophon: instead of promising results to others, the ācārya testifies from his own experience, which lends the closing guarantee its authority. That guarantee is dhruvam — 'fixedly, certainly' — the firm last word of the stava, transferring the steadiness of the meditation itself onto the certainty of its fruit. The reward, sarva-siddhi ('all attainment'), is deliberately total and open-ended, gathering the protective benefits catalogued earlier (freedom from fear, spirits, affliction) into the single comprehensive promise with which a smaraṇa fittingly ends.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encapsulates the Jain understanding that meditative remembrance is itself efficacious: dhyāna directed at a Tīrthaṅkara who has perfected dhyāna becomes a path to one's own perfection. The phrase dhyāna-sthitaṁ presents Pārśva in the posture of a kevalin absorbed in pure contemplation, the very state the practitioner aspires to. By placing the Lord 'in the heart' the verse aligns with the corpus-wide principle that the seed-mantra and the Lord's name must be interiorized, not merely recited. Sarva-siddhi here should be read on two registers: the laukika (worldly removal of obstacles, the smaraṇa's protective purpose) and the lokottara (spiritual accomplishment culminating in liberation) — the hymn's open-ended 'all' embraces both.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For today's reciter the verse offers a method, not just a blessing: end the recitation by quietly visualizing Pārśva, calm and meditative, seated within your own heart, and let your stillness imitate his. The first-person labhe invites you to claim the practice as your own. The closing dhruvam is meant to dispel doubt — recite it as an act of confidence, trusting that steadfast, heartfelt meditation yields its fruit.

crossReferences.text: Jīrāpallī Pārśva Stava v.11 (colophon naming Śrī Merutuṅgasūri) and v.14 — the paired author-signature verses
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: v.8 ('mantra-king awakening, embodied, in the heart') — the heart-meditation motif this verse fulfils
Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.27–9.46 on dhyāna as the means of nirjarā and accomplishment

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit–English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon) — entries dhruva/dhruvam, puras, siddhi, dhyāna, sthita
sources.consultedFor: Glosses and roots for dhruvam (adv. 'certainly'), puraḥ (indecl. 'before'), siddhi, dhyāna, sthita, √labh.

sources.title: Parshvanatha (23rd Tīrthaṅkara)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Pārśva-prabhu as the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara invoked in the verse.

sources.title: Jirawala (Jīrāvalā / Jīrāpallī Pārśvanātha tīrtha, Sirohī, Rājasthān)
sources.consultedFor: Identification of the Jīrāpallī/Jīrāvalā Pārśva tīrtha and its Merutuṅga association.

sources.title: Siddhi — concept of accomplishment/attainment in Jain and Indian thought
sources.consultedFor: Twofold (laukika/lokottara) sense of sarva-siddhi as worldly success and spiritual fulfilment.

notes: This colophon verse is in Sanskrit (not Prakrit). Two printed/OCR readings are honoured but flagged: (1) 'શ્રીમત્યાર્શ્વ' (śrīmaty-ārśva) is read as śrīmat-pārśva-prabhoḥ ('of the glorious Lord Pārśva'), the loss of the initial પ being a likely print/OCR variant; (2) 'હદિ' is read as hṛdi ('in the heart'), the ṛ-vocalic mātrā being absent in the source. The verse self-attributes authorship to Śrī Merutuṅgasūri, matching v.11; per the preface, some modern sources dispute this attribution — unresolved, with the book treated as the golden source. sarva-siddhi is intentionally rendered with both worldly and spiritual scope. 'sūrīndraḥ' is nominative apposition to the subject Merutuṅga; 'śrīmat-pārśva-prabhoḥ' is genitive governed by 'puraḥ'.
